talk_metadata.talk_title: Recent advances in treatment of pancreas cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "When you look at chemotherapy in the second-line setting, the response rate is 10 to 15%... we almost doubled the response rate with adagrasib"
Speaker said: "A hazard ratio of 0.4, we have not seen these numbers in pancreatic cancer before"

Speaker said: "I was honestly a little bit tense over the last year... because I participated in the Napoli 1 trial, which led to the approval of 5-FU liposome, Irinotecan, and just negative trial after negative trial"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Only ~1.5% of pancreatic cancer patients harbor KRAS G12C mutations, limiting population benefit
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted a response rate of ~33% in KRAS G12C basket trial but emphasized it applies to a very small patient subset

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: KRAS G12C inhibition in pancreatic cancer
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all pancreatic cancer patients without KRAS G12C biomarker pre‑screening (basket design)
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering for the small KRAS G12C subset

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted that the pocket discovered in 2013 is specific to G12C and does not address the more prevalent KRAS G12D/V/R mutations
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing a pan‑KRAS approach without robust biomarker‑selected enrollment dilutes signal

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed a 33% response rate for KRAS G12C inhibitors (a clear improvement over chemotherapy) but concluded that KRAS remains an “undruggable target” for the majority of patients

key_data_gaps: Limited biomarker‑stratified analysis beyond KRAS G12C

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for robust KRAS mutation screening to match patients with appropriate KRAS inhibitors (G12C, G12D, G12V, G12R)
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that KRAS G12C occurs in only ~1.5% of patients and that basket trials enroll many KRAS‑wild‑type patients
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate NGS data to predict KRAS allele‑specific response and prioritize patients for pan‑KRAS or allele‑specific agents

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify synergistic partners (e.g., immunotherapy, chemotherapy) for KRAS inhibitors to improve durability
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned ongoing combinations of duraxon rasib with gem/nab‑paclitaxel and noted durability concerns
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model pathway interactions to suggest rational combination regimens and predict resistance mechanisms

cited_competitors.name: Amgen sotorasib
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as an NCCN‑guideline KRAS G12C inhibitor alongside adagrasib

cited_competitors.name: Revolution Medicine (deraxone rasip)
cited_competitors.context: Presented phase‑I data with 36% response rate and 8.8 mo PFS

unresolved_questions: What are the mechanisms of acquired resistance to KRAS G12D/V/R inhibitors in pancreatic cancer?
Can short‑burst KRAS inhibition provide long‑term disease control without continuous dosing?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "About a third of the detected non‑canonical peptides showed zero evidence of translation in any healthy tissue"
Speaker said: "Cancer‑restricted cryptic antigens were highly enriched for immunogenicity, with about a quarter positive in ex‑vivo T‑cell assays"
Speaker said: "Targeting multiple distinct cryptic antigens nearly eradicated patient‑derived organoids in long‑term assays"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Antigen heterogeneity within tumors limits efficacy of monotherapy TCR cells
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker noted that TCR001 delayed tumor progression but did not completely eradicate tumors

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker demonstrated that many cryptic peptides are shared across patients, suggesting off‑the‑shelf potential but also risk of cross‑reactivity with rare normal tissue expression
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single cryptic antigen may dilute clinical signal due to intra‑tumoral heterogeneity

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that cancer‑restricted cryptic peptides are presented on HLA‑I and immunogenic, yet concluded that they are still “just a step back” compared to classic mutation‑derived neoantigens for therapeutic development

key_data_gaps: No in‑human clinical data on cryptic‑antigen TCR therapies
Limited assessment of off‑target toxicity in normal tissues

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage CrisPRO to prioritize combinations of multiple cryptic antigens to overcome heterogeneity
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker reported that targeting two cryptic antigens nearly eradicated organoids
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model antigen co‑expression networks and suggest optimal multi‑antigen TCR cocktails

crispro_opportunity.description: Demonstrate computational advantage of CrisPRO over standard neoantigen pipelines by including dark‑proteome peptides
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that mutation‑derived neoepitopes were few compared to abundant non‑canonical peptides
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO integrates proteogenomic data to capture both canonical and cryptic antigens for vaccine design

cited_competitors.name: Tyler Jacks lab (MIT)
cited_competitors.context: Collaborated on organoid models and ribosome profiling

unresolved_questions: What is the precise ribosomal or translational machinery alteration that drives non‑canonical ORF translation in pancreatic cancer?
Can cryptic antigen‑specific TCRs achieve durable tumor eradication in vivo when combined with checkpoint blockade?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "When we turn on CA19‑9 in the whole mouse, we only see signs of inflammation in the pancreas"
Speaker said: "Three days of CA19‑9 is sufficient to dramatically expand the fibroblast population and ramp up proliferation"
Speaker said: "Blocking CA19‑9 with antibody 5B1 normalizes histologic architecture and reduces both collagen deposition and Treg infiltration"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: High circulating CA19‑9 levels act as a sink, reducing antibody availability at tumor sites
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted that patients with the highest CA19‑9 levels had increased clearance of the antibody

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Neutralization of CA19‑9 glycan to remodel TME
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Did not stratify patients by baseline CA19‑9 serum concentration, leading to variable drug exposure
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline CA19‑9 titer to adjust dosing
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underestimation of efficacy due to high‑CA19‑9 patients diluting overall response

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the antibody binds a widely expressed glycan on many proteins, leading to off‑target sink effects
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Lack of patient selection based on CA19‑9 burden limits therapeutic window

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated that CA19‑9 drives immunosuppressive fibroblast and Treg expansion, yet concluded that CA19‑9 is merely a biomarker without functional relevance in early disease

key_data_gaps: No clinical efficacy data for CA19‑9 blockade in combination with checkpoint inhibitors
Mechanistic link between CA19‑9‑modified fibulin‑3 and EGFR signaling not validated in human tumors

crispro_opportunity.description: Target the CA19‑9‑fibulin‑3‑EGFR axis to overcome TME‑mediated resistance to KRAS inhibitors
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed that CA19‑9 modification of fibulin‑3 enhances EGFR activation and drives IL‑1α/TGF‑β production
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model glyco‑protein interaction networks to predict combinatorial targets that disrupt this axis

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a quantitative CA19‑9 serum threshold to select patients for antibody or combination therapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that high CA19‑9 levels caused rapid antibody clearance and reduced efficacy
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate pharmacokinetic modeling with serum biomarker levels to optimize dosing strategies

cited_competitors.name: BioNTech (acquired 5B1 antibody from Madvax)
cited_competitors.context: Developed the CA19‑9 blocking antibody used in the discontinued trial

cited_competitors.name: Madvax
cited_competitors.context: Original developer of the 5B1 CA19‑9 antibody

unresolved_questions: Which CA19‑9‑modified proteins besides fibulin‑3 are critical for TME remodeling?
Can short‑term CA19‑9 blockade synergize with KRAS inhibitors to improve early‑stage outcomes?
